_meta.description: Structured log of strategic and operational decisions with rationale. One of the highest-value registries — captures why, not just what.

records.title: Haiku as default model; Sonnet reserved for document upload and complex cases only
records.summary: All AI calls default to claude-haiku-4-5-20251001. Sonnet (claude-sonnet-4-6) is used only when document upload is present or the routing table explicitly calls for it.
rationale: Haiku delivers sufficient quality for structured letter generation at 10-20x lower cost than Sonnet
$150/month budget cap requires cost discipline from the start
MA-COST-001 3-bucket rule: default to cheapest model that satisfies quality bar
Sonnet quality advantage is measurable only in complex reasoning and document comprehension tasks
records.notes: Enforced via MODEL_ROUTER in src/lib/generate-letter.ts. Hard-coded model strings elsewhere are a violation.

records.title: All Anthropic API calls routed through generateLetter() abstraction — never bypass
records.summary: generateLetter() in src/lib/generate-letter.ts is the single routing boundary for all AI calls. No direct SDK calls from page or component code.
rationale: Single enforcement point for PII scrubber, model selection, output caps, cost logging, budget tripwires
Allows model swap, provider swap, or routing logic change without touching call sites
Prevents gradual accumulation of direct API calls that bypass governance controls
MA-SEC-002 Control 1: PII scrubber never optional — abstraction makes enforcement structural
records.notes: Violation of this rule is a critical security event per MA-SEC-002.

records.title: PII scrubbing mandatory before every external model call — never optional
records.summary: All free text containing user-submitted content passes through src/lib/pii-scrubber.ts before any Anthropic API call. This step cannot be bypassed.
rationale: Healthcare data is HIPAA-adjacent — PII exposure is a critical liability
User trust is the product's core asset; privacy violations destroy it irreversibly
Regulatory risk: even without explicit HIPAA coverage, YMYL health data carries FTC and state-level privacy obligations
Structural enforcement (via generateLetter abstraction) is more reliable than developer discipline
records.notes: Four-layer privacy model in SYSTEM.md Section 4. Layer 2 = PII scrubber.

records.title: Phase-gated growth — unlock conditions, not calendar dates
records.summary: No feature, market, or channel expansion without meeting defined unlock conditions. Growth is earned by hitting measurable signals, not by elapsed time.
rationale: Prevents premature scaling of unvalidated features
Solo-founder operability constraint: complexity that requires a team is premature
Protects budget cap by preventing speculative infrastructure build
Signal-based gating creates measurable, accountable milestones
records.notes: Parking Lot in Notion holds all phase-gated items. Check before building anything in the lot.

records.title: GEO-first content strategy — 134-167 word self-contained answer blocks
records.summary: All SEO content uses GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) structure: 134-167 word answer blocks, direct-answer first, FAQPage schema, citability score ≥70/100.
rationale: AI search citation growing 527% faster than traditional organic per GEO-01 analysis
ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews are primary discovery channels for target audience
Self-contained answer blocks serve both AI extraction and traditional featured snippets
Citability scoring provides measurable quality gate before publish
records.notes: GEO-01 agent enforces citability ≥70/100. GEO-03 writes denial-code SEO pages. CNT-01 is the YMYL compliance layer.

records.title: Context registry as JSON files in Phase 1 — no database migration until 500+ records or performance degrades
records.summary: The context registry is lightweight JSON files in /context_registry/. Migration to SQLite occurs at 500+ records. Migration to Supabase occurs at Signal 1 traffic or concurrent writes needed.
rationale: JSON preserves launch momentum — no database infrastructure to provision
MA-CTX-001 migration path is signal-triggered, not calendar-triggered
Schema is intentionally SQL-compatible, making migration structural not transformational
Solo-founder operability: JSON is editable by anyone without database access
records.notes: Review trigger: any single registry file exceeds 500 records OR query performance degrades.

records.title: AI actor character for patient story videos — founders remain anonymous
records.summary: Patient story videos use HeyGen AI avatars (English + Spanish). Founders do not appear on camera. AI actor character maintains brand voice without personal exposure.
rationale: Founder privacy and safety: healthcare advocacy can attract hostile actors
AI avatars scale to ES content without separate production pipeline
Character consistency across dozens of videos without scheduling constraints
Gate 0 eligible immediately (tools live + first SEO article = unlock condition)
records.notes: HeyGen avatar selection: test 3 EN + 3 ES candidates with same script before committing. Story bank needs 3 approved stories before Month 3 YouTube launch.

records.title: Proprietary Data Engine: stub friction_events in Phase 1, publish nothing until Phase 3 thresholds met
records.summary: The friction_events table (MA-SUP-DAT-001) is a Phase 1 schema obligation, not a Phase 3 feature. Collection begins at launch; publication is gated on statistical thresholds (1,000+ events for first content) and attorney review of insurer-naming framing. The competitive moat accrues only if the clock starts now.
rationale: Data collection must begin before the dataset is useful — a competitor starting two years later starts with zero data
friction_events schema must be stubbed in Phase 1 to avoid a breaking migration later when outcome columns are added in Phase 2
Publishing prematurely with small samples destroys credibility and creates legal exposure — gate on thresholds in MA-SUP-DAT-001 §4
claim_amount_range is the highest-leverage optional field: converts pattern data into a financial impact figure citable in press outreach
Four-layer privacy architecture (structured input, PII scrubber, context firewall, stateless API) makes collection safe within the existing compliance model
Attorney HIPAA-adjacent risk assessment of insurer+procedure_type+state field combination is required before Phase 3 publication — not before collection
This is the same authority pattern used by NerdWallet, Glassdoor, and Zillow — moat is user relationship and time investment, not technology
records.notes: Review trigger: Phase 2 Month 3-4 — activate outcome follow-up flow. Phase 3 gate: 1,000+ friction events AND attorney sign-off. See MA-SUP-DAT-001 §4 for full publication thresholds. Migration 019 creates the table stub.

records.title: Cornerstone sequencing rule — no cluster pages before parent cornerstone is live and indexed
records.summary: Cluster pages under a cornerstone topic MUST NOT publish before the parent cornerstone guide is live and indexed. 10 cornerstone guides are phased across Phase 1 (1 guide) through Phase 4 (9 guides). Each cornerstone anchors a sub-cluster.
rationale: Publishing cluster pages before the parent cornerstone wastes authority signal — clusters inherit trust from the cornerstone, not the other way around
Fragment topical authority prematurely and Google sees disconnected pages, not a topic authority
Phase 1 gate: Complete Guide to Insurance Claim Denials is required before any denial cluster pages publish
10-guide library covers the full patient advocacy topic space — phased rollout prevents premature infrastructure build
records.notes: Full cornerstone library: (1) Insurance Claim Denials [Phase 1 launch gate], (2) Medical Billing Disputes [Phase 4 Wave 1], (3) Patient Rights in Hospitals [Phase 4 Wave 1], (4) Medicaid Appeals [Phase 4 Wave 2], (5) Medicare Appeals [Phase 4 Wave 2], (6) Prior Authorization [Phase 4 Wave 2], (7) No Surprises Act [Phase 4 Wave 3], (8) Nursing Home Rights [Phase 4 Wave 3], (9) Hospital Complaints [Phase 4 Wave 3], (10) Charity Care [Phase 4 Wave 4]. Each requires 4,000–6,000 words, 8–12 sections, 5+ authoritative citations, Kate sign-off, and GEO template compliance.

records.title: GEO template is a Phase 1 publish standard — not a Phase 4 initiative
records.summary: Every page published from Day 1 must include 8 GEO elements: (1) direct-answer block within first 200 words, (2) H2 question-format headers, (3) numbered step lists for process content, (4) definition boxes for technical terms, (5) 5+ authoritative citations, (6) FAQPage/HowTo/Article schema markup, (7) summary box at page bottom, (8) visible review date. Content generation system must output these as standard fields. Non-compliant pages receive geo_compliance = false flag and enter refresh queue.
rationale: AI search citation is a Phase 1 opportunity — waiting until Phase 4 to comply means all Phase 1/2/3 content was published without it and requires retroactive refresh at cost
GEO and traditional SEO are complementary, not competing — same page structure serves both Google featured snippets and AI citation extraction
Content Refresh Agent will retroactively enforce compliance, but clean-slate compliance is always cheaper than retroactive
Research shows direct-answer blocks in first 200 words are primary AI extraction target — this is the single highest-leverage formatting decision
records.notes: Schema implementation plan: Organization schema site-wide; Article schema on every article (datePublished, dateModified, reviewedBy editorial board); FAQPage schema on denial code pages, policy pages, rights pages; HowTo schema on all process guides; MedicalWebPage schema where applicable. GEO compliance rate target: 100% new publishes, 80% retroactive within 6 months of Phase 4 activation.

records.title: Agent deployment schedule revised — Content Refresh Agent to Phase 2, Data/Insights Agent to Phase 3, CMO modules accelerated
records.summary: MA-SEO-SUP-001 §10 revises the Board of Agents deployment sequence. No new agents added; scope expansions and earlier activations only. Content Refresh Agent moves from Phase 4 → Phase 2 Sprint 1. Data/Insights Agent moves from Phase 5 → Phase 3. CMO Outreach Module moves from Phase 4 → Phase 3. CMO Spanish Track moves from Phase 5 → Phase 4. GEO Module moves from Phase 4 → Phase 1 (hardwired into content generation from Day 1). CMO Surge Detection is a new sub-module within CMO Agent, activates Phase 4.
rationale: Content published in Phase 1/2 without a refresh agent has no monitoring — Phase 4 activation leaves months of content unmonitored and creates technical debt
Annual Outcomes Report (Section 7) requires 9–12 months of structured outcome data before Month 12 publish gate — Data/Insights Agent must come online at Phase 3 to meet this timeline
Backlink outreach (Section 4) requires CMO Agent tracking from Month 4 — Phase 4 activation misses the first outreach window
GEO compliance is a Day 1 standard per dec_0011 — the GEO Module must be active from first publish
records.notes: Original deployment gates in MA-AGT-001 are superseded by MA-SEO-SUP-001 §10 for the five affected agents. All other agent gates in MA-AGT-001 remain unchanged. Signal-gated agents (MKT-01, MKT-02, MKT-03, PRD-01) are unaffected by this revision.

records.title: Anonymous institutional attribution model — MyAdvocate Editorial Board carries E-E-A-T authority, not named founders
records.summary: Sarsh and Kate remain anonymous in all public-facing content. E-E-A-T authority is carried by the MyAdvocate brand and Editorial Board, not by individual attribution. Every published article must include the official attribution statement: 'Reviewed by the MyAdvocate Editorial Board, which includes a licensed medical professional with over 20 years of clinical healthcare experience.' Site schema uses Organization type, not Person type.
rationale: Healthcare advocacy can attract hostile actors — founder anonymity is a legitimate and defensible safety decision
E-E-A-T does not require personal attribution; institutional attribution satisfies the framework when supported by process documentation
Nine required trust infrastructure pages create institutional credibility without personal exposure
The editorial board model is a structural protection: the board survives any individual personnel change
records.notes: Attribution block placement: below article title, above first paragraph; secondary at bottom above disclaimer. Format: italicized, gray background box or border. All 9 trust infrastructure pages must be live before launch (MA-SEO-SUP-001 §2 — Phase 1 launch gate). Hard rule: NEVER reference Sarsh or Kate by name in any public-facing content. This aligns with and extends the anonymity decision in dec_0007 (AI actor character for video content).

records.title: Content refresh cadence formalized — four-tier threshold system with refresh_priority_score 0–100
records.summary: All content pages carry a refresh_priority_score (0–100). Pages above 70 enter refresh queue automatically; pages above 90 escalate to founder dashboard as urgent. Four tiers: Tier 1 (programmatic denial codes) 18–24 month review; Tier 2 (cluster editorial) 9–12 month; Tier 3 (cornerstones, policy, state rights) 6 month; Surge (policy surge articles) 3 month post-publish. Refresh triggers include stale age, >20% impression drop, >30% CTR drop, statute changes, attorney or Kate flags.
rationale: YMYL content that goes stale is a legal and ranking liability — structured refresh prevents drift
Score-based prioritization ensures highest-risk pages (Tier 3, statute-backed) are refreshed first
Phase 4 refresh-before-expand rule prevents cluster bloat: no new pages in a cluster with outstanding refresh queue
Refresh-to-traffic lift rate metric validates the system — if refreshed pages don't recover traffic, the refresh approach needs revision
records.notes: Page metadata fields required: content_tier (1/2/3/surge), last_reviewed_date, source_dependency_type (statute_backed / regulatory_backed / editorial), refresh_priority_score (0–100, auto-calculated). Score weights: stale age vs tier threshold 30%, performance decay composite 25%, source dependency risk 20%, Tier 3 multiplier 1.5x 15%, tool CTA integrity 10%. Phase 2 Sprint 1 obligation: deploy these metadata fields before Content Refresh Agent activates.

records.title: Append-only Supabase migrations — never edit past migrations
records.summary: All Supabase schema changes require a new migration file. Past migrations are immutable. supabase migration new <name> is the only allowed workflow.
rationale: Editing past migrations creates drift between local and production environments
Append-only enables reliable rollback and team onboarding
Pre-commit hook in guard-migrations.sh enforces this at the tooling layer
Supabase managed infrastructure requires migration history integrity
records.notes: 15 migrations as of v21. Next: migration 016 (scrub_records for MA-SOC-002).

records.title: Agent hierarchy replaces flat agent model — 5-director org chart with boss-over-boss reporting
records.summary: The flat .claude/agents/ model from MA-AGT-001 is superseded by a hierarchical organization: Founder Chief of Staff → 5 Directors (Engineering, Content & Compliance, Growth & Ops, Finance & Risk, Trust & Compliance) → 25+ specialists and 8 runtime-mirror specs. Existing Phase 1 agents (GEO-01/02/03, DEV-01/02/03, CNT-01) are preserved in legacy-phase1/ subfolder; their functions are absorbed by the new hierarchy agents. Only the Founder Chief of Staff communicates routine summaries to the founder; specialists report to directors only.
rationale: PMP already describes an agent operating model — the hierarchy makes it repo-native and enforceable
Flat model creates 'everyone talks to the founder' chaos as agent count grows past 7
Director layer converts specialist noise into portfolio-level updates
Runtime-mirror specs keep build-time implementation aligned to V4 Scoring Service agent weights
Config layer (hierarchy YAML, reporting matrix, handoff rules) provides machine-readable org chart for n8n and scoring service integration
Phase gates and signal-gated discipline from MA-AGT-001 are preserved — growth agents remain deferred until traffic signals
records.notes: MA-AGT-002 canonical doc. Tier 1 install complete 2026-03-19. Tier 2 (event taxonomy wiring, runtime-mirror activation) targets Sprint 2. Tier 3 (dashboard panels, n8n-triggered OpenHands) targets Month 2+. Agent-to-task mapping in config/agent_runtime/agent-task-mapping.json.

records.title: OpenHands adopted as supervised coding agent — bounded task execution in branches with PR review
records.summary: OpenHands serves as a supervised junior developer for bounded implementation tasks. It works in Git branches, returns PRs for founder review, and never has autonomous merge authority. Three-stage rollout: (1) manual task specs for 2-3 current-sprint items, (2) GitHub issue-driven workflow with label convention, (3) n8n-triggered low-risk automation. OpenHands complements the agent hierarchy — hierarchy is organizational structure, OpenHands is execution capacity.
rationale: Execution capacity is the primary bottleneck for a two-person team — not organizational structure
Bounded task specs + PR review = safe delegation without losing control
Several Phase 2 Sprint 1 items are perfect OpenHands candidates: content validation CLI, pre-launch check command, server/client boundary audit
OpenHands Playbook provides governance framework aligned with existing MA-SEC-002 controls
Stage 3 (n8n-triggered) creates CTO Sentinel → auto-fix pipeline for test failures and low-risk issues
records.notes: Stage 1 starts immediately. First 3 task specs: content validation CLI, pre-launch engineering check, server/client boundary audit. Review at 30 days to decide Stage 2 activation. Playbook task specs 1-6 are stale (already completed). Active tasks: 7, 9, 10.
